This involves repeatedly fitting a weighted linear regression of a working response variable on the covariates; each regression yields a new value of the parameter estimates, which in turn give new working responses and weights.

The '.632 estimator' is designed to alleviate this bias. It is defined by 0.368 × err + 0.632 × 0Err(1). The derivation of the .632 estimator is complex; intuitively it pulls the leave-one out bootstrap estimate down toward the training error rate, and hence reduces its upward bias.

uncertainty, by sampling from the training data.

Using this, the relative overfitting rate is defined to be a quantity that ranges from 0 if there is no overfitting (0Err(1) = err) to 1 if the overfitting equals the no-information value γ̂−err. First we define γ to be the no-information error rate: this is the error rate of our prediction rule if the inputs and class labels were independent. An estimate of γ is obtained by evaluating the prediction rule on all possible combinations of targets yi and predictors xi'.

Consider a variation of the bootstrap, called the parametric bootstrap, in which we simulate new responses by adding Gaussian noise to predicted values: y∗i = μ̂(xi) + ε∗i; ε∗i ∼N(0, σ̂²); i = 1, 2, . . . , N.

The bootstrap method provides a direct computational way of assessing
uncertainty, by sampling from the training data. Here we illustrate the
bootstrap in a simple one-dimensional smoothing problem, and show its
connection to maximum likelihood.

Denote the training data by Z = {z1, z2, . . . , zN}, with zi = (xi, yi),
i = 1, 2, . . . , N. Here xi is a one-dimensional input, and yi the outcome,
either continuous or categorical.

both of these minimizations are instances of the
maximum likelihood approach to fitting.
The score function is defined by ℓ̇(θ; Z) = ∑ᴺᴵ₌₁ ℓ̇(θ; zi) where ℓ̇(θ; zi) = ∂ℓ(θ; zi)/∂θ. Assuming that the likelihood takes its maximum in the interior of the parameter space, ℓ̇(θ̂; Z) = 0.

The information matrix is I(θ) = −∂²ℓ(θ; Z)/∂θ∂θᵀ. When I(θ) is evaluated at θ = θ̂, it is often called the observed information.

The distribution (8.25) with τ→∞ is called a noninformative prior for θ. In Gaussian models, maximum likelihood and parametric bootstrap analyses tend to agree with Bayesian analyses that use a noninformative prior for the free parameters.

In this chapter we provide a general exposition of the maximum likeli-
hood approach, as well as the Bayesian method for inference.

Distributions on functions are fairly complex entities: one approach is to use a Gaussian process prior in which we specify the prior covariance between any two function values μ(x) and μ(x').

The implicit process prior for μ(x) is hence Gaussian, with covariance kernel K(x, x') = cov[μ(x), μ(x')] = τ·h(x)ᵀΣh(x').
Distributions on functions are fairly complex entities: one approach is to use a Gaussian process prior in which we specify the prior covariance between any two function values μ(x) and μ(x').

The EM algorithm is a popular tool for simplifying difficult maximum likelihood problems. These problems are ones for which maximization of the likelihood is difficult, but made easier by enlarging the sample with latent (unobserved) data. This is called data augmentation.

Our observed data is Z, having log-likelihood ℓ(θ; Z) depending on parameters θ. The latent or missing data is Zm, so that the complete data is T = (Z, Zm) with log-likelihood ℓ0(θ; T), ℓ0 based on the complete density.

Having defined a Bayesian model, one would like to draw samples from the resulting posterior distribution, in order to make inferences about the parameters. In this section we discuss the Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) approach to posterior sampling.

In this chapter we provide a general exposition of the maximum likeli-
hood approach, as well as the Bayesian method for inference.

related techniques for model averaging and improvement, including com-
mittee methods, bagging, stacking and bumping.

The Gibbs sampling procedure alternatively simulates from each of these distributions and when the process stabilizes, provides a sample from the desired joint distribution. More formally, Gibbs sampling produces a Markov chain whose stationary distribution is the true joint distribution, and hence the term 'Markov chain Monte Carlo.'
